ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper offers a notable and concrete new perspective by explicitly incorporating recursion into neural programming training traces (NPI) and tying this to a verification strategy based on covering base cases and reduction rules — an idea not present in the cited prior neural program learners. The formulation addresses a well-studied domain (neural program induction), so the problem itself is not novel, but the technique of forcing recursive structure in traces and arguing that this makes verification tractable is a non-trivial extension that clearly differentiates the contribution from non-recursive NPI baselines. An expert would find the approach informative, though the novelty is tempered by reliance on trace supervision and by applicability limits acknowledged by the authors. | B(4): The paper offers a notable and concrete novelty by introducing explicit recursion into neural programming architectures (NPI) and showing that recursive execution traces yield near-perfect generalization on several algorithmic tasks (addition, bubble sort, topological sort, quicksort). The combination of training with recursive traces plus a practical verification approach (covering base cases and reduction rules) clearly differentiates it from prior neural-programming work and provides a tractable path to provable generalization. While the idea of using recursion is conceptually straightforward and depends on strong supervision (full execution traces), the empirical demonstrations across multiple algorithms and the verification framing make this a meaningful and informative advance for experts in the area.

The formulation addresses a well-studied domain (neural program induction), so the problem itself is not novel, but the technique of forcing recursive structure in traces and arguing that this makes verification tractable is a non-trivial extension that clearly differentiates the contribution from non-recursive NPI baselines. An expert would find the approach informative, though the novelty is tempered by reliance on trace supervision and by applicability limits acknowledged by the authors.

The combination of training with recursive traces plus a practical verification approach (covering base cases and reduction rules) clearly differentiates it from prior neural-programming work and provides a tractable path to provable generalization. While the idea of using recursion is conceptually straightforward and depends on strong supervision (full execution traces), the empirical demonstrations across multiple algorithms and the verification framing make this a meaningful and informative advance for experts in the area.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are stated early, with a clear methodological narrative (modifying NPI traces to induce recursion) and relevant experiments on several algorithmic tasks. Notation and presentation are reported as consistent and writing quality is good, and figures/tables/examples appear informative for the synthetic tasks. From a formalist perspective, the presentation could be improved by explicitly stating the verification procedure as formal lemmas/theorems with proofs or formally specified completeness assumptions for the verification set—this omission weakens clarity about the claimed "provable" generalization. Otherwise the flow and clarity are strong, with only minor presentation gaps around formalization of the verification claims and limits. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from motivation through method (recursively annotated NPI traces) to experiments on multiple algorithmic tasks and a conclusion about verifiable generalization. The main contributions are stated early and experiments across addition, sorting, and graph algorithms make the claims concrete; notation appears consistent and figures/tables/examples are informative. Writing quality is generally good, though a few presentation details (e.g., omission of code availability and further clarity on the practical limits of the verification procedure) could be expanded for full reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are stated early, with a clear methodological narrative (modifying NPI traces to induce recursion) and relevant experiments on several algorithmic tasks. Notation and presentation are reported as consistent and writing quality is good, and figures/tables/examples appear informative for the synthetic tasks. From a formalist perspective, the presentation could be improved by explicitly stating the verification procedure as formal lemmas/theorems with proofs or formally specified completeness assumptions for the verification set—this omission weakens clarity about the claimed "provable" generalization. Otherwise the flow and clarity are strong, with only minor presentation gaps around formalization of the verification claims and limits.

The main contributions are stated early and experiments across addition, sorting, and graph algorithms make the claims concrete; notation appears consistent and figures/tables/examples are informative. Writing quality is generally good, though a few presentation details (e.g., omission of code availability and further clarity on the practical limits of the verification procedure) could be expanded for full reproducibility.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical results are strong and that the verification recipe is a useful, practically attractive idea, but both note the paper stops short of formal, general proofs and does not precisely characterize when a finite verification set suffices. Reviewer A emphasizes the theoretical gap and unstated assumptions about enumerability of base cases and reductions, while Reviewer B tolerates these as limitations to broader applicability. Given solid experiments but incomplete formal guarantees and scalability concerns, a cautious score of 3 reflects sound empirical work tempered by the theoretical shortcomings.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by convincing empirical evidence on synthetic algorithmic tasks and by a proposed verification recipe, with sensible baselines (non-recursive NPI) included. However, as a theorist I find a gap between the asserted ‘‘provable’’ perfect generalization and the presented material: the summary does not report formal theorems or complete proofs that recursion plus a finite verification set guarantees correctness for all inputs, nor a formal characterization of when the verification set is complete. The verification argument appears to rely on an (often unstated) assumption that base cases and reduction rules are finitely enumerable and exhaustively checked, which is not justified for many realistic domains. Overall the experiments are sound but the theoretical claims require explicit formal statements and proofs or precise conditions under which the verification is tractable to merit a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claim—that adding recursion to NPI yields near-perfect generalization on several algorithmic tasks—is convincingly supported by empirical results (addition, bubble sort, topological sort, quicksort) showing 100% correctness from small training sets and comparisons to non‑recursive and partially recursive NPIs. The paper's verification argument (cover base cases and reductions to obtain provable generalization) is practically attractive and makes testing tractable, but it stops short of a general formal proof for all task classes and acknowledges scalability limits for large/infinite domains. Experimental methodology is broadly sound (appropriate baselines and multiple algorithms), though reliance on synthetic traces and full execution supervision is a limitation that weakens claims about broader applicability. Overall the technical evidence supports the core claim, with only minor gaps around formal completeness and applicability to less-structured domains.

main_claims: Incorporating recursion into neural programming architectures enables learned neural programs to generalize perfectly to inputs of unbounded complexity for several algorithmic tasks.

By training the Neural Programmer-Interpreter (NPI) with recursive execution traces, the model can learn recursive programs that empirically achieve 100% correctness on valid inputs after training on very small numbers of examples.
Recursion makes verification tractable: given a verification set covering all base cases and reduction rules, one can provide formal proofs of perfect generalization for the learned neural programs.
_summary.methodology: Modify the NPI training traces to include explicit recursive calls so the NPI learns recursive versions of programs (addition, bubble sort, topological sort, quicksort); then verify correctness by testing all base cases and reduction rules to obtain provable generalization.
baselines: non-recursive NPI (same architecture without recursive traces)
references to prior neural programming models (e.g., Neural GPU, Differentiable Neural Computer, Neural Programmer, Pointer Network)
metrics: accuracy (correctness) on randomly generated inputs of varying size
limitations: Verification procedure may be infeasible for tasks with very large or infinite input domains (e.g., raw MNIST digits or speech) because base cases and reduction rules can be prohibitively large or infinite.
Current training requires providing full execution traces with explicit recursive structure; reducing this supervision is left to future work.
_summary.topic_area: Neural program induction / neural programming architectures

reviewer_a.name: Formalist Theorist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in theoretical machine learning and formal methods with a PhD in theoretical computer science; experience proving generalization and correctness properties of learning algorithms.
traits: Demands formal statements (theorems/lemmas) with proofs for any claim of 'perfect' or 'provable' generalization; deducts points if such claims rely only on empirical verification.

Penalizes methods that claim tractable verification via ad-hoc verification sets instead of provably complete verification procedures; subtracts significant merit when verification completeness is not formally established.

Prefers clear reductions to formal models (e.g., reductions to known decidable fragments or formal program semantics); awards points for explicit formalization of assumptions and limits of the approach.
Requires comparison to symbolic/program-synthesis baselines when the paper addresses program correctness/generalization; deducts points if these comparisons are missing or superficial.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Critical, precision-oriented reviewer who prioritizes formal guarantees and explicit assumptions; will score highly only when theoretical claims are accompanied by rigorous proofs or formally stated limitations.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Systems Empiricist
reviewer_b.background: Senior applied ML researcher with extensive experience building neural architectures for program induction and structured prediction; background in engineering scalable models and empirical evaluation.
traits: Rewards strong empirical demonstrations of generalization (especially near-perfect performance on algorithmic tasks from very small training sets); gives positive bias when experiments show robustness across multiple algorithms.

Values methods that make verification/testing tractable in practice (e.g., small verification sets covering base cases and reductions) even if full formal proof is not provided; awards extra credit for practical verifiability.
Expects comprehensive experimental ablations and multiple algorithmic tasks; rewards papers that evaluate on several representative algorithms and show consistent gains.
Penalizes absence of reasonable baselines but is forgiving if the proposed approach demonstrates clear empirical advantages over standard neural-programming models.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Empirical, system-focused reviewer who favors practical demonstrations of scalability and generalization; will score favorably when experiments are thorough and show clear, reproducible gains even in absence of complete formal proofs.
